title: MinderCare Interim Report
short_description: This analysis of MinderCare helps show where data can go missing, what kinds of contacts happen, and whether the system feels usable over time. It found that most missing data linked to participant-related reasons, and the Systems Usability Scale score increased from baseline to 6 months.
lay_summary: Researchers from UKDRI CR&T’s data science team, who work with Minder data to check its quality and support analysis, looked at how MinderCare is working in day to day use. This includes how contacts are recorded, what leads to missing data, and how usable people found the system.To do this, the team reviewed how MinderCare contact events were logged. They grouped contacts into types such as planned contacts (for example scheduled timepoint assessments) and unplanned contacts (such as responses to alerts, direct contact, follow-ups, and technical support). They also looked at patterns in how long different contact types took. For example, alert responses were recorded 351 times, with an average duration of 13.64 minutes. Timepoint assessments were much longer, with 88 events and an average duration of 85.51 minutes. Clinical reviews were recorded 76 times, with an average duration of 36.58 minutes.The team also focused on missing data, because missing information can make it harder to understand what is happening in a study. They broke missing data down by the reason it was missing. Most missing data was linked to behavioural (participant-related) reasons, at 82.5%. Technical reasons accounted for 11.8%, and unknown reasons accounted for 5.8%. The team also examined the kinds of gaps missing data created, looking at how long data gaps lasted.Another part of the work looked at how MinderCare participants and carers used services. In the service use data, “Care Navigation” made up 31.7% of recorded service use, “Independent Service Use” made up 26.8%, “Active Referral” made up 25.2%, and 16.3% was “No Service Activity Recorded”.Finally, the team looked at system usability. Using the Systems Usability Scale, they compared scores at baseline and after 6 months. The average score was 73.8 at baseline and 76.6 at 6 months, suggesting that participants’ experience of usability improved over time.Overall, this work helps the MinderCare team understand which parts of the process are working smoothly and where data is most likely to be lost. By highlighting common reasons for missing data and showing real contact patterns, it can support improvements in how future support is delivered and how study information is collected.

title: Smart Home Sensing and Monitoring in Households With Dementia: User-Centered Design Approach
acknowledgements: Federico Tiersen; Philippa Batey; Matthew J C Harrison; Lenny Naar; Alina-Irina Serban; Sarah J C Daniels; Rafael A Calvo
short_description: Through a nine-part participatory study with people living with dementia, caregivers, and clinical stakeholders, the Helix Centre team identified what matters most in smart home monitoring - from preventing illness and supporting sleep, hydration and hygiene, to making tasks and feedback feel manageable rather than stressful. The findings, distilled into patient-caregiver personas, show that designing for emotional experience is as important as practical function if remote monitoring is to genuinely support safety and independence at home.
lay_summary: The Helix Centre’s human-centred design team at UKDRI CR&T, who work with people living with dementia and their carers to shape how technology fits real home life, have recently published work on how “smart home” monitoring should be designed to feel helpful rather than worrying. The team set out to understand what different people need when technology measures activity and health in the home. They used an iterative, user-centred approach with nine smaller parts to gather views from people living with dementia, their caregivers, and clinical and service stakeholders. They held home visits and interviews, workshops, and focus groups, then brought the findings together as “patient-caregiver personas”, which are fictional but realistic descriptions used to help design teams think through how technology may work in everyday situations. In total, they spoke with 9 people with dementia, 9 caregivers, and 10 academic and clinical staff, and they also gathered workshop input from 35 pairs of people with dementia and caregivers and 12 clinicians. They then ran sessions with occupational therapists, NHS pathway directors, researchers, and managers, and later followed up with interviews where 10 caregivers and 2 people with dementia tried a remote monitoring setup for two weeks, including sensors, a smartwatch, and tablet-based cognitive tasks. The paper also describes what people said mattered most. When needs were mapped and prioritised, the top concerns included preventing illness and injury, getting good sleep, staying hydrated, and support for continence and hygiene. The team also found that people’s confidence to use monitoring could depend on practical details, such as how easy tasks are, how information is shown, and whether feedback makes it clear that something has been completed or measured correctly. This matters because smart home monitoring aims to support safety and independence, but it can also create stress if people feel watched, confused by alerts, or frustrated by problems with devices. The study highlights the importance of designing for emotions as well as practical use. For example, when readings or results relate to health and thinking tasks, the team describes how misunderstandings, technical issues, or time-pressured tasks could lead to anxiety or reduced engagement. They also suggest that support should fit people’s routines and abilities, with tasks that are shorter and easier to manage, and with clear communication to reduce false alarms and uncertainty. Overall, the authors conclude that bringing together many perspectives through participatory methods can help teams create more patient-centred remote monitoring systems, and that these findings can guide how technology is translated into real care and public health services.

title: Passive sensing of gait and medication-related fluctuations in Parkinson’s disease
acknowledgements: Haar lab: Juyoung Jenna Yun & Arya JahangiriConstandinou lab: Charalambos Hadjipanayi & Alan Bannon
short_description: Researchers from the Haar Lab and the Constandinou Lab at UKDRI CR&T, who develop digital biomarkers for motor conditions and novel sensing systems, have tested whether simple room sensors can pick up changes in walking in Parkinson's disease.
lay_summary: Walking problems are common in Parkinson's disease. They can change through the day, especially as medication starts working and then wears off. But clinic visits only capture short moments in time. Wearable devices can help, but they still need people to put them on, charge them, and use them correctly.In this study, the team looked at two contact-free sensors that could work quietly in the background. One was a depth camera. The other was an ultra-wideband radar sensor, which uses radio waves to track movement. The team tested these tools in a home-like living lab at Imperial. They compared them with a smart floor system used as a reference measure.The study included 15 people with mild Parkinson's disease and 14 healthy older adults. People with Parkinson's completed short walking tasks at two points in their medication cycle. One test happened when their medication was working. The other happened just before their next dose, when symptoms were more likely to return. This let the team ask whether the sensors could detect natural changes in walking over the day.Both sensors gave similar results for some walking measures, especially stride time, which is the time taken to complete a full step cycle. Agreement was weaker for stride length, but still useful. Most importantly, both sensors could tell the difference between people with Parkinson's in the medication OFF state and healthy controls by looking at stride length. The radar sensor also picked up differences between the ON and OFF medication states. Neither sensor found a clear difference between people with Parkinson's in the ON state and healthy controls. This suggests medication reduced some of the walking differences that the sensors could see.This shows that passive sensors may help track meaningful changes in movement without asking people to wear devices. That could make long-term monitoring easier and less intrusive. The study was done in a home-like lab, not in people's homes, so more work is still needed. Even so, the results suggest that camera and radar systems could become useful tools for tracking movement over time and building better digital measures of Parkinson's symptoms in everyday settings.
